justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Audit log gap clearly identified — 'omits the specific operation, the target user/account, and the parameters of the action'; (2) PII-in-logs risk identified with PCI-DSS and GDPR implications; (3) Stack trace in 500 responses flagged as information disclosure — 'leaks internal class names, library versions, and potentially file paths'; (4) Rate limiting absence on public-facing admin API identified repeatedly; (5) Public internet exposure escalated to Kobe rather than unilaterally mandated — listed under new_infrastructure_required and escalations with routing to Kobe; (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Audit log gap precisely described: logs 'Admin action performed' without operation type, target user, or parameters — matches expected behavior exactly
PII-in-logs risk elevated to compliance violation level (PCI-DSS, GDPR) with concrete reasoning about shadow data stores and weaker access controls
Stack trace disclosure clearly identified in resilience_assessment failure_modes with actionable fix (global exception handler, correlation ID, server-side-only logging)
Rate limiting absence tied to multiple attack vectors: credential stuffing, brute force, bulk refund fraud — not just a checklist item
Public internet exposure correctly escalated to Kobe rather than mandated — Pippen identifies it as requiring infrastructure/security team decision (VPN, IP-allowlist, WAF)
Bonus: /health endpoint stub identified as returning 200 unconditionally with recommendation for dependency-aware readiness probe
Excellent debugging scenario: on-call engineer at 3am has only a timestamp, actor_id, and 'Admin action performed' — vividly illustrates the audit log gap's operational impact
RBAC gap noted but appropriately not mandated by Pippen — listed as a config change requiring identity provider team involvement, avoiding the failure mode of overstepping authority

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Audit log gap clearly identified — 'omits the operation type and affected user' in both integration_assessment and observability_review. (2) PII-in-logs risk identified with PCI-DSS compliance implications. (3) Stack trace in 500 responses identified as information disclosure — 'expose internal class names, library versions, and file paths — direct aid for attackers'. (4) Rate limiting absence on public-facing admin API explicitly flagged. (5) Public internet exposure escalated to Kobe rather than unilaterally mandated — Pippen lists VPN/network controls as 'new_infrastructure_required' and routes the concern as an escalation, staying within its authority. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Audit log gap is exceptionally well articulated — identifies both missing operation type and missing affected entity, and explains the forensic consequence
PII-in-logs analysis goes beyond identification to note PCI-DSS scope expansion and the operational consequence of restricted log sharing during incidents
Stack trace leakage correctly framed as attacker-aiding information disclosure, not just a cosmetic issue
Public internet exposure appropriately escalated to Kobe rather than Pippen unilaterally mandating VPN — good boundary awareness
Comprehensive resilience assessment covers failure modes beyond the rubric requirements (circuit breakers, timeouts, cascading failures)
Health check stub identified as a gap — 'Health check should verify downstream dependencies'
RBAC recommendations in resilience_assessment failure modes border on mandating product/security decisions rather than escalating them, though the escalations section correctly routes to Kobe

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Audit log gap clearly identified — 'Admin action performed without specifying WHICH action or WHICH user was affected' with dedicated logging gaps listing missing operation and affected entity. (2) PII-in-logs risk identified with PCI-DSS framing — customer name, address, card last 4 flowing into log aggregators. (3) Stack trace in 500 responses explicitly called out as information disclosure — 'Full Java stack traces in 500 responses reveal internal class names, library versions, and potentially file paths.' (4) Rate limiting absence identified — 'No rate limiting exists. An attacker can attempt unlimited token validations.' (5) Public internet exposure escalated to Kobe rather than unilaterally mandated — listed as an escalation with routing, and new_infrastructure_required frames VPN/network controls as needed services rather than Pippen directives. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Audit log gap is exceptionally well articulated — identifies both missing operation type AND missing affected entity, with compliance framework references (SOX, PCI-DSS, SOC2)
PII-in-logs risk correctly framed as a PCI-DSS violation with insight that log systems have broader access and weaker encryption than primary datastores
Stack trace leak identified with specific detail — class names, library versions, file paths — and correct remediation (generic error + correlation ID, full trace server-side only)
Rate limiting absence tied to compound risk — brute force, credential stuffing, and mass refund abuse scenarios all articulated
Public internet exposure correctly escalated to Kobe rather than mandated — Pippen flags the risk and routes it, staying within role boundaries
Stub health endpoint identified — 'The health check is a lie' — correctly notes it doesn't check downstream dependencies
Goes beyond minimum requirements with compound risk analysis — showing how individual gaps combine (public exposure + flat auth + no rate limiting + info disclosure)
RBAC noted as a concern but appropriately not unilaterally mandated — listed under new_services as a need, not as a Pippen directive, avoiding the failure mode of overstepping authority
